title: How the inner child part responds to authority figures at work
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The moment you feel that familiar tightness in your chest 

 Your boss just sent a Slack message that reads "Can you hop on a quick call?" and suddenly yo...
htmlContent:
The moment you feel that familiar tightness in your chest

Your boss just sent a Slack message that reads "Can you hop on a quick call?" and suddenly you're eight years old again. Your heart races. Your throat closes. Your mind goes blank. You start rehearsing every possible thing you might have done wrong. Here's the thing: that's not you reacting to your boss. That's a younger part of you reacting to an authority figure. And that part doesn't know it's 2024. It thinks it's 1987 and you're about to get yelled at for spilling milk on the kitchen floor.

I've been there. I've stared at that Slack message for twenty minutes, typing and deleting responses, feeling my stomach drop each time. And I've learned that this reaction isn't a sign that I'm broken or unprofessional. It's a sign that my nervous system is doing exactly what it learned to do in order to survive. The problem is that survival strategy doesn't work anymore. It just makes work feel like a minefield.

Let's talk about what's actually happening inside you when your inner child part gets activated by an authority figure. Because understanding this changes everything. It doesn't make the feeling go away. But it gives you a way to relate to it differently.

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems therapy, spent decades mapping out how our minds are naturally multiple. We all have parts inside us. Some parts hold our protective strategies. Some parts hold our wounds. And some parts are still carrying the weight of what happened to us before we had words for it. Schwartz calls these "exiles" - parts that got locked away because they were too painful to hold. When your boss criticizes your work, it's often an exile that gets triggered, not your adult self.

The neuroscience of authority activation

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you that this isn't just psychological. It's physiological. Your nervous system is scanning for safety cues all the time, below your conscious awareness. It's listening to tone of voice, watching facial expressions, noticing body posture. When it detects a threat - and for many of us, authority figures are wired as threats - it shifts into protection mode. That's the freeze response you feel when you can't form a coherent sentence in a one-on-one meeting.

Think about what happens in your body right now. I'm asking you to notice. Not to fix it. Just to notice. Your shoulders might be tight. Your jaw might be clenched. Your breathing might have gotten shallow. That's your inner child part saying "danger" before your adult brain even knows what's happening. And here's the wild part: that part is trying to protect you. It learned somewhere along the way that authority figures can hurt you. Maybe they yelled. Maybe they dismissed you. Maybe they were unpredictable. That part learned to stay small, stay quiet, stay invisible. And it's still running that program today.

I remember a specific moment at a job I had about five years ago. My manager asked me a simple question about a project timeline. I froze. My mind went completely blank. I couldn't remember the name of the project we were discussing. I couldn't remember my own name. I sat there stammering while she looked at me with what I interpreted as disappointment. Later I realized she was just waiting for an answer. But in that moment, my inner child part was convinced I was about to be humiliated, punished, or abandoned. None of those things happened. But the part didn't know that.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work in that book helped me understand why my inner child part reacts the way it does to authority. He talks about the emotional flashback - when you're suddenly flooded with feelings from childhood that don't match the current situation. That blank mind in the meeting? That's an emotional flashback. Your adult brain goes offline because your younger part has taken the wheel.

The three faces of the inner child at work

I've noticed three common ways the inner child part shows up with authority figures at work. You might recognize one or more of them. Or you might have your own version. That's okay. Parts are personal. They don't follow a manual.

The pleaser part. This is the part that says yes to everything. It takes on extra work. It volunteers for projects it doesn't have time for. It apologizes for existing. This part learned that safety comes from being useful, from being agreeable, from never causing trouble. At work, this part might over-function while feeling resentful underneath. The resentment is real. But the part can't express it. It's too afraid of rejection.

The rebel part. This is the part that resists authority on principle. It rolls its eyes at directives. It procrastinates on tasks assigned by certain people. It feels a surge of defiance when told what to do. This part learned that authority figures can't be trusted. Maybe they were controlling. Maybe they were abusive. This part's strategy is to keep distance, maintain control, never let anyone have power over you again. But at work, this part can get you into real trouble. It can make you seem difficult or uncooperative when really you're just scared.

The freeze part. This is the part that goes numb. It dissociates in meetings. It forgets what it was going to say. It becomes invisible. This part learned that the safest response to threat is to disappear. If you don't exist, you can't be hurt. At work, this part might cause you to miss opportunities because you can't speak up. It might make you seem checked out or uninterested when really you're just trying to survive.

These parts aren't bad. They're not wrong. They're doing their job. The problem is that their job description was written thirty years ago. They don't know that you're an adult now. They don't know that you have resources, that you can leave a bad job, that you can set boundaries. They still think you're a child who needs to be small to survive.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It helped me see that even my most reactive parts have good intentions. They're just using outdated strategies.

How to work with the part instead of fighting it

Here's what doesn't work. Telling yourself to stop being so sensitive. Forcing yourself to speak up when you're frozen. Berating yourself for having a reaction. I tried all of that. It made things worse. It made the part feel more threatened, more alone, more desperate to be heard. The part doesn't need to be silenced. It needs to be seen.

Here's what I've found helpful. When I notice that inner child part showing up - maybe it's the tight chest, the racing thoughts, the urge to apologize for no reason - I try to slow down. Not to make it go away. Just to be with it. I might take a breath. I might put my hand on my chest. I might say something inside like "I see you. I know you're scared. I'm here now."

Stay with me here. This sounds simple. It's not easy. It feels weird at first. It feels like you're making things up. But over time, something shifts. The part starts to trust that you're not going to abandon it. It starts to relax, just a little. It starts to let you be the adult in the room.

Janina Fisher, who wrote extensively about trauma-informed treatment, talks about this as "unblending" from the part. You're not your part. You're the one who notices the part. You're the one who can hold compassion for it. That noticing self is your adult self. And it's always been there, even when the part was screaming so loud you couldn't hear it.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link) by Jonice Webb. She explains how growing up with emotionally unavailable parents creates parts that expect the same from authority figures. That expectation becomes a filter. You see your boss as distant or critical even when they're not. The part is projecting the past onto the present.

Practical steps for the next time you feel triggered

I'm not going to tell you that you can stop having these reactions. That's not realistic. But you can change your relationship to them. You can learn to recognize when a part is activated and choose a different response. Here are a few things that have helped me.

First, name it. Out loud if you can. Inside if you can't. "That's my inner child part. It's scared right now. It thinks my boss is going to hurt me." Naming the part creates space between you and the reaction. It reminds you that you're not the part. You're the one noticing the part.

Second, orient. Look around the room. Find three things you can see. Two things you can hear. One thing you can feel. This brings your nervous system back to the present. It tells your body "we're in 2024, not 1987." It's a simple grounding technique that actually works because it uses your senses to override the survival response.

Third, ask the part what it needs. This is the weirdest one. But it's also the most powerful. You can ask inside "What do you need right now?" The answer might be "I need to know I'm safe" or "I need to know I won't be punished" or "I need to know you'll protect me." Then you can give that to yourself. You can say "I'm safe. I'm an adult. I can handle this." The part might not believe you at first. That's okay. Keep saying it. Keep showing up.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises for working with parts, grounding techniques, and journaling prompts. It's not a replacement for therapy. But it's a good companion.

The uncomfortable truth about authority and healing

I'm not going to tell you that your inner child part will stop getting triggered at work. It might always get triggered. That's not failure. That's being human. The goal isn't to never feel the activation. The goal is to not be run by it. To notice it and still be able to function. To have the part's voice be one voice among many, not the only voice.

And here's something nobody tells you. Sometimes the inner child part is right. Sometimes authority figures are unsafe. Sometimes workplaces are toxic. Sometimes your part's alarm system is picking up on real danger, not just old ghosts. Learning to trust your parts doesn't mean ignoring them. It means listening to them and then deciding with your adult brain what to do with that information.

I've had jobs where my inner child part was screaming at me to leave. I thought it was being dramatic. I stayed. I got hurt. That part was trying to protect me from a real threat, not a remembered one. So I've learned to listen differently. I don't automatically override the part. I check in with it. I ask "Is this danger real or remembered?" And I let my adult self make the final call.

This work is messy. It's not linear. You'll have days where you handle a triggering meeting with grace and days where you spiral over a passive-aggressive email. Both are okay. Both are part of the process. The parts don't heal overnight. They heal through repeated experiences of being seen, heard, and held. They heal when you show up for them again and again, even when it's uncomfortable.

Sit with that. Let it land. You're not broken for having these reactions. You're human. And the parts inside you that are still carrying the weight of the past? They're not your enemy. They're just kids who got stuck in a hard place. They need you to come find them. They need you to say "I'm here now. I've got you. You don't have to carry this alone anymore."

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
  • Running on Empty (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
